primary_topics: Why we have so many steels (alloy content and product forms)
Cost of alloying elements (nickel, chromium, molybdenum, vanadium)
Aircraft carrier weight gain and HSLA-65 study
Diffusion coefficients of H and C in alpha- vs gamma-iron
Stout & Doty Weldability of Steels reference tables
Seawolf submarine HY-100 hydrogen cracking incident
Fracture mechanics applied to in-service inspection decisions
Cape Air / Cessna failure analysis consulting
cases_referenced: Jerry Milgram America's Cup center board (HY-80 / 4340)
Newport News HSLA-65 carrier weight study (1996)
Charles Street MBTA station (1910) welding procedure
notes: Lecture continues Tom's hydrogen-cracking arc into the question of why so many steels exist and how alloy content interacts with hardenability, weldability, and cost. The Seawolf HY-100 case is the central technical narrative, with Aloha Airlines and Cape Air as fracture-mechanics analogues. Captioner garbled many proper nouns and metallurgical terms (Aloha, Jominy, martensite, NAVSEA, Doty, Pelloux); all corrected silently per convention 10.
